author: @mgalyean
text:
On a more loosely related note addressing how we grossly underestimate the resilience of nature and grossly overestimate our understanding of nature (usually for political reasons):
Marine life produces vastly more hydrocarbons per year than human oil spills..and another naturally occurring marine species consumes all that "oil", and greatly mitigates human oil spills, every year. Removing politics from science is a worthy endeavor and we should never underestimate nature or overestimate our understanding for political or romantic reasons.
https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2015/10/151005151417.htm

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Marty G i get the feeling this has pushed something of a button for you lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Marty G I mean that in a nice way mate - misunderstanding based on romanticism is a button for me too - thanks for the balance

replies.author: @mgalyean
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith, you didn't press it as you didn't call them "islands of plastic" lol, which is why I wrote "loosely related note".  My posts were for public consumption in a public forum where many viewers may be imagining "islands of plastic", not directed at you personally.  Basically, I anytime I hear about the "plastic gyres" it makes me wish more people kept up with real research and the realities of the complexities and resilience of the environment, that's all.  Keep making these great vids!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Marty G no worries mate - i always appreciate your posts

replies.author: @craigtevis1241
replies.text:
+Marty G 
Others grossly downplay the damage done to nature by human activities and underestimate our understanding of nature, often for political reasons. If you go to the article referenced in Science Daily you'll read this:

"The researchers caution that their results do not in any way diminish the enormous harm caused by oil spills. Although some microorganisms are known to break down hydrocarbons in oil spills, they cannot repair the damage done to marine life, seabirds and coastal ecosystems."
http://www.cam.ac.uk/research/news/bacteria-in-the-worlds-oceans-produce-millions-of-tonnes-of-hydrocarbons-each-year#sthash.vLrGGaWT.dpuf

author: @mgalyean
text:
On a loosely related note:
Mealworms east styrofoam
 http://www.popsci.com/mealworms-can-safely-devour-plastics
Marine bacteria eat plastic, fishing line etc
 http://www.nature.com/news/2011/110328/full/news.2011.191.html

replies.author: @ScottSextonandDavidLewis
replies.text: +Marty G Cause for pause Rob?  Recent articles promote graphene as a medical anti-bacterial substance (freely acting as nano-knifes that puncture bacterial cell walls), but *particularly* when in the presence of salt water. If graphene bio thermoplastic decomposition is catalyzed by salt water, could the released graphene kill beneficial bacteria in the oceans such as the marine bacteria Marty G mentions (not to mention other beneficial bacteria such as the e coli strains utilized in most animal intestines)? I can't help but wonder if the SWAG effect (Salt Water And Graphene) is an operator in all three of these conditions:  decomposition of bio graphene thermoplastic, electric power generation by passing salt water over graphene, and graphene antibacterial action greatly increased by salt water presence.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Scott Sexton oh - now you have raised an issue - i am going to have to have a think about that one mate - nice one - cheers

replies.author: @strongforce8466
replies.text: +Scott Sexton  I have read nano particles are dangerous for that reason because it's so small it can even damage the DNA, now that's a bit worrisome lol

replies.author: @mgalyean
replies.text: +Thomas Syxe Wow.  "I have read", "nano", "dangerous", "DNA", "worrisome".  Who can argue with that?  I'm reminded of the dire predictions of multi-generational DNA damage that would be caused by a nuclear meltdown at a power plant.  To this day, science can't find any significant multi-generational DNA damage at Three Mile Island, Chernobyl, Nagasaki, or Hiroshima.  DNA is *tough* stuff with lots of redundancy.  When someone demands you respond with fear, always questions their motives.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Marty G i tend to think exactly the same thing mate

author: @mgalyean
text: While plastic is overrated and over-utilized as a material in many cases, it turns out that naturally occurring insects and bacteria do break down most of them already and they really don't "last forever".  .  Additionally, the "plastic gyres" are horribly exaggerated.  There are no islands of plastic etc.  That said, anyone who throws plastic crap in the ocean or on the ground is a very poor steward of all that we've been gifted.   My biggest peeve with plastic is when a formerly aluminum or steel part is replaced with plastic of the same dimensions when common sense wold dictate that to handle the same loads it would need to be thicker with bigger radius fillets and perhaps fiber reinforced.  Instead it is the same part, but breaks far sooner.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Marty G nice post mate - cheers
